name: Buck Clayton
abstract: Wilbur Dorsey "Buck" Clayton (November 12, 1911 – December 8, 1991) was an American jazz trumpeter who was a member of Count Basie's orchestra. His principal influence was Louis Armstrong, first hearing the record "Confessin' that I Love You" as he passed by a shop window.

quotes.text: Well that was my first time ever to play Carnegie Hall and it was the time that
quotes.caption: Buck Clayton recalls his first Carnegie Hall performance

quotes.text: Yes. You see, so my mother didn't want me to get into that kind of life and she never did accept it until I played Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Mother accepted his career after Carnegie Hall performance

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: People like Frankie Newton, Pete Brown, great innovative players, Chu Berry, Roy Eldridge, Buck Clayton, wonderful trumpet players, who could play rings around Wynton Marsalis. Completely swept under the carpet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Jang
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee narrates a historical event involving Buck Clayton and Li Jinhui ('he and Buck Clayton in 1935... they collaborated'), but does not claim a personal relationship with Buck Clayton.

about_quotes.speaker: Jake Hanna
about_quotes.evidence: You told her that in late ‘59 you were playing in Storyville with Buck Clayton and Jimmy Rushing? Yes. That’s right.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jake Hanna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Temperley
about_quotes.evidence: He was a good friend. ... I miss Buck Clayton because he was a real dear friend of mine.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Temperley interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: George Wein
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Buck Clayton and Vic Dickinson and Doc Cheatham and Buddy Tate
about_quotes.doc_title: George Wein interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Harry "Sweets" Edison
about_quotes.evidence: sitting next to Lester Young — gee whiz, what a thrill you know. Jo Jones. Walter Page. Freddie Green, Buck Clayton, sitting next to him — you © Fillius Jazz Archive -8- know it wasn’t but three trumpets, Buck Clayton, Ed Lewis and myself
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry "Sweets" Edison interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995
